title: Measures to retain and recycle black mass from electric vehicle batteries within the EU
bodyHtml:
In a letter received from the Turkish Exporters Assembly, referring to a communication from the Ministry of Trade of the Republic of Türkiye, it was conveyed that a written question regarding the protection and recycling of electric vehicle battery waste in the EU, submitted to the European Commission on April 8, 2025, by Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) Thomas Pellerin-Carlin (S&D) and Lídia Pereira (EPP), was answered by European Commissioner for Environment, Water Resilience and Competitive Circular Economy, Jessika Roswall, and published on July 1, 2025.

In the aforementioned written question, it was stated that improving the recycling of electric vehicle batteries in the EU is of great importance for increasing the Union's autonomy and resilience in critical raw materials. It was emphasized that converting the main substance of battery waste — "black mass," which contains valuable raw materials such as nickel, cobalt, and lithium — into economic value holds strategic importance, particularly for the battery industry, the defense sector, and the circular economy. However, concerns were raised that exporting battery waste to non-EU countries is currently easier than transporting it within the EU. One reason cited for this situation was the lack of harmonized regulations. The Commission’s recent decision to classify battery waste as hazardous waste was considered a step in the right direction, aiming to prevent easy exports to non-OECD third countries. Nonetheless, it was noted that a significant portion of waste is being exported to South Korea, an OECD member.

Accordingly, the question posed to the Commission inquired about the steps the Commission plans to take to harmonize rules on the intra-EU shipment of battery waste and to develop an economic model to retain and recycle raw materials within the EU.

In her response on behalf of the Commission, Roswall stated:

  • For waste destined for disposal, hazardous waste, and most mixed waste sent for recovery, the prior notification and consent procedure applies, requiring the relevant operator to obtain approval in advance from the competent authorities of all concerned countries (including the countries of dispatch, destination, and transit) before the shipment takes place;
  • The new Waste Shipment Regulation will simplify this process by transitioning to an electronic system, and as of May 2026, a central system will ensure smooth information exchange;
  • A hub will be developed to enable document and information exchange between the central system, local systems operated by some competent authorities, and software provided by commercial vendors;
  • An implementing regulation concerning the interoperability of these systems, which will allow for the electronic transmission and exchange of information and documents, will be published in the near future;
  • Regarding recycling, under Article 72(3) of the Battery Regulation, in order to export waste batteries or their components outside the EU, the exporter must provide documentation approved by the competent authority of the destination country demonstrating that the process will be carried out under conditions equivalent to those set out in the Battery Regulation and other relevant EU legislation concerning the protection of human health and the environment;
  • It was indicated that all these regulations and implementations will enhance the economic model for the recycling of battery waste within the EU.

In this context, it was noted that the relevant written question and its response can be accessed via the following link:
